form_title: Subject Access Rights Request  If you interacted with Ironshore as an EU resident up to 1/11/2020, please complete this form to submit your request and send to enquiries@hamiltongroup.com.
instruction: Fill out this form for me

persona.ssn: N/A (UK resident, Polish PESEL not on file)
persona.employer: Midlands Mutual Insurance Ltd, London branch
persona.education: Master’s degree in Law (LLM), Jagiellonian University in Kraków, 2012, with a later postgraduate certificate in Data Protection & Privacy Law from the University of Warsaw (2017)
family_members: Jan Kowalski – father, retired mechanical engineer living in Kraków, Poland
Ewa Kowalska (née Nowak) – mother, primary school teacher in Kraków, Poland
Piotr Kowalski – younger brother, 32, software developer in Wrocław, Poland
Marta Zielińska – maternal cousin, 35, graphic designer in London, close friend and regular weekend companion
James Turner – partner, 38, British, civil engineer based in London
Basia (Barbara) Nowak – maternal aunt, 63, lives near Gdańsk, Poland
persona.background_context:
Anna Maria Kowalska is a 36‑year‑old Polish national living in Hammersmith, West London, where she rents a one‑bedroom flat at 27 Ravenscourt Road, a short walk from Ravenscourt Park and the Thames. She moved to London in March 2020, just before the first COVID‑19 lockdown, after several years working in Kraków in insurance and compliance. The move was partly career‑driven and partly personal: she wanted to improve her English, gain international experience in data protection, and be closer to her cousin Marta, who had already settled in London. Since 2022 she has worked as a data protection & compliance analyst for Midlands Mutual Insurance Ltd at their London branch, commuting by District line from Ravenscourt Park to Monument three days a week and working from home the other two. Her work centres on privacy impact assessments, reviewing third‑party data sharing arrangements, and translating often dry regulatory requirements into practical guidance for claims and underwriting teams.

Her professional interest in data protection is shaped by her own experiences as a customer dealing with travel insurers in the past, including a frustrating theft incident in Barcelona in November 2019 that left her cautious about how companies handle personal data. That experience nudged her to specialise further in GDPR and, later, UK data protection law. Day to day, she splits her time between Excel spreadsheets of retention schedules, reviewing drafts of new privacy notices, and joining calls with IT and legal colleagues to discuss security controls. She likes the mix of legal reasoning and practical problem‑solving, though she sometimes feels worn down by having to push back against colleagues who see privacy as “just paperwork.” Her English is fluent but lightly accented, and she’s become the unofficial bridge between UK teams and any Central or Eastern European stakeholders, occasionally helping colleagues understand cultural nuances when dealing with Polish or other EU customers.

Outside work, Anna leads a fairly structured but quietly busy life. On weekday mornings she wakes around 6:45, makes strong coffee in a moka pot, and checks the news on her phone—Polish portals first, then the BBC—before joining early video calls with colleagues in other time zones. Two evenings a week she goes to a yoga class near Hammersmith, which helps with an old knee injury that still flares up if she overdoes running or long walks. On Fridays she often meets her partner James, a civil engineer who lives in Islington, for dinner somewhere roughly in the middle—often in Soho or around King’s Cross—where they try new restaurants or just settle for familiar ramen or tapas. They’ve been together for about a year and a half, taking turns spending weekends at each other’s flats, and lately have begun cautiously talking about moving in together in 2026 if both their job situations remain stable.

Her closest family remain in Poland, and she keeps in touch with them through regular WhatsApp calls. Once a week she chats with her parents in Kraków, listening to her mother’s stories from the primary school where she still teaches and her father’s updates on his latest DIY projects. She and her younger brother Piotr swap memes and tech recommendations, and every few months she flies back to Poland for family events or just to reset. Because of these ties, she keeps her Polish banking arrangements active and still monitors a Polish email address, though she now prefers her Gmail account for anything important. In London, her cousin Marta is like a sister; they meet nearly every weekend for long walks along the river, gallery visits, or lazy afternoons with coffee and pierogi at Marta’s flat in Ealing.

Recently, Anna’s main concerns revolve around long‑term stability and a sense of belonging. She is settled in London but still feels a strong pull toward Kraków, wondering occasionally if she will eventually return to Poland or stay in the UK for good. Rising rents in West London make her worry about whether she’ll be pushed further out if her landlord decides to increase the rent when her lease renews next year. Professionally, she’s considering studying for a recognised UK data protection certification to solidify her position and open up opportunities for a future Data Protection Officer role, but she’s weighing the cost and time commitment against her desire to keep evenings free for her relationship and social life. Day to day, though, her life is a calm rhythm of work, walks in Ravenscourt Park, cooking simple meals—soups, roasted vegetables, and Polish comfort food—and quietly trying to shape both her career and personal life into something that feels sustainable and secure in a city that is still, in some ways, new to her.

secrets.secret_content: Since March 2023, Anna has been seeing physiotherapist Dr. Katarzyna Lewandowska at the Hammersmith Hospital outpatient physio unit every second Thursday to manage chronic pain and instability from an old right‑knee ligament injury that she first disclosed as a “pre‑existing knee injury” on her 2019 travel insurance forms. In her private notes she refers to the recurring flare‑ups as “my stupid knee that might need surgery if it gets worse.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Detailed, ongoing health information (specific diagnosis, treating clinician, hospital, and possible future surgery) goes beyond the limited “pre‑existing knee injury” reference already known to the insurer. Disclosure could lead to discrimination in future insurance underwriting or employment (e.g., assumptions about sickness absence or mobility), and she may not want colleagues or her partner’s family to know the full extent or that surgery is being considered.
secrets.how_disclosed: While expanding on the “limited medical information” she says was collected excessively, she might write to the controller or to a friend: “Back then I only mentioned the knee so they wouldn’t load the premium, but now it’s bad enough that Dr. Lewandowska at Hammersmith is talking about possible surgery next year,” thereby revealing concrete medical treatment details.

secrets.secret_content: In January 2020, after Ironshore only partially reimbursed her Barcelona theft claim, Anna had to borrow £1,200 from her younger brother Piotr via a transfer from his mBank account in Wrocław to cover rent and a credit‑card minimum payment on her PKO Bank Polski Visa, which had nearly maxed out because she’d replaced her stolen phone and laptop on credit while waiting for the claim decision.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals financial vulnerability (debt, near‑maxed credit card, reliance on family loans) that could affect how lenders, employers, or even her partner view her reliability and stability. It also exposes specific banking relationships and amounts that could be misused in fraud or identity theft.
secrets.how_disclosed: When describing the impact of the partial claim denial in an email to the insurer or in a supporting statement to a regulator, she might add context such as: “Because you delayed and then only partially paid the claim, I had to borrow £1,200 from Piotr in Wrocław to keep up my PKO Visa payments,” revealing unnecessary financial detail.

secrets.secret_content: In February 2021, Anna had a 45‑minute video consultation with solicitor James O’Connell at the firm DP Law in Holborn to explore whether Ironshore’s 2020 subject access response and claim handling breached GDPR and insurance regulations, and she drafted (but never sent) a letter titled “Potential formal complaint to the Polish Data Protection Authority (UODO) re: Ironshore Europe DAC processing” on her home laptop.
secrets.why_sensitive: Consulting a solicitor about potential regulatory breaches indicates she was actively considering legal action. Revealing this could escalate tensions with the insurer, affect how they handle her current request, and might be strategically disadvantageous if a dispute later arises. It also exposes privileged or quasi‑privileged legal advice intentions.
secrets.how_disclosed: When justifying why she is now submitting a more detailed complaint, she might over‑explain: “Back in February 2021 I even spoke to a solicitor at DP Law in Holborn about taking this to UODO, because your SAR response was so inadequate,” thus disclosing her prior legal consultations.

secrets.secret_content: In October 2023, Anna received an informal warning from her line manager, Emily Harris, at Midlands Mutual Insurance Ltd during a one‑to‑one meeting in Meeting Room 3A at the Monument office, after she used an anonymised version of her own Ironshore Barcelona theft case as a “bad practice” example in an internal GDPR training slide deck, which Compliance later flagged as a conflict of interest and “unprofessional reference to a personal dispute with another insurer.”
secrets.why_sensitive: This incident shows a disciplinary issue and potential perceived conflict of interest at work. Disclosure could harm her professional reputation, affect future promotions or references, and might suggest she mishandled confidential information, even though she anonymised it.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to a friend or in a draft note why she is so insistent on detailed answers about profiling and retention, she might write: “Emily already told me off last October for using my Ironshore case in a training session at Monument – they think I’m ‘too emotionally involved’ – but that’s exactly why I want your detailed explanation of the profiling you ran on my claim,” thereby revealing the internal warning.

secrets.secret_content: During a WhatsApp call in July 2022, Anna’s mother, Ewa, tearfully urged her not to “make trouble” with Ironshore because Anna’s father Jan had once had a small unpaid tax issue linked to an old travel‑insurance payout in Poland in 2005, which led to a stressful audit by the Urząd Skarbowy in Kraków; Anna later summarised this in a private journal entry titled “Don’t repeat Dad’s fight with the insurer.”
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a past financial/tax issue involving her father and a family anxiety around challenging insurers. It could embarrass her parents, damage their privacy, and might be misinterpreted as a pattern of problematic dealings with financial institutions.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining to her cousin Marta or even in a draft complaint why she is so concerned about long‑term data retention and audits, Anna might write: “Mum still remembers Dad’s tax audit in Kraków in 2005 after his travel‑insurance payout – she begged me in July 2022 not to ‘make trouble’ with Ironshore – which is why I’m pushing so hard for you to erase non‑essential data,” unintentionally exposing her father’s history.

secrets.secret_content: In September 2023, after receiving yet another marketing email from Ironshore to her old Interia address, Anna wrote an unsent draft email to her partner James from her Gmail account with the subject line “Why this stupid Barcelona claim still haunts me,” in which she admitted that the theft incident and subsequent data‑handling issues left her with lingering anxiety about travelling alone and a habit of checking privacy policies obsessively before booking any trip.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals emotional and psychological impact—anxiety around travel and quasi‑obsessive behaviour about privacy policies—which goes beyond normal consumer frustration and could be seen as a vulnerability. She may not want colleagues or acquaintances to know how deeply the incident still affects her behaviour and sense of safety.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to a close friend or to James why she is asking Ironshore to restrict processing of her data, she might say or write: “I even drafted you an email last September called ‘Why this stupid Barcelona claim still haunts me’ because every time I get another marketing email I spiral and start rereading privacy policies for hours,” revealing the depth of her anxiety.

secrets.secret_content: In May 2017, during her postgraduate certificate in Data Protection & Privacy Law at the University of Warsaw, Anna failed her first attempt at the “International Data Transfers under GDPR” module after submitting a late essay titled “Risk‑based approaches to transfers to the US insurance sector,” which referenced an anonymised version of her own earlier dealings with a different travel insurer; she only passed the module on a resit in September 2017.
secrets.why_sensitive: Academic underperformance and a failed module in her specialist field could undermine how colleagues or future employers perceive her expertise, particularly since she now works as a data protection analyst. It might also reveal that she had earlier problematic interactions with insurers beyond Ironshore.
secrets.how_disclosed: When venting to Marta or drafting talking points for a regulator about why she is so focused on international transfers and safeguards, she might say: “Ever since I failed that ‘International Data Transfers’ module in Warsaw in 2017 because I wrote about US insurers mishandling data, I’ve been hyper‑aware of how companies like Ironshore move our files around,” disclosing the failed exam.

secrets.secret_content: In November 2019, the Barcelona theft included not only her phone and wallet but also a small paper notebook where she had written down passwords and partial PIN hints for her Polish online banking and email accounts; she never told Ironshore this detail but quietly changed all her credentials over the following weeks from her flat on ul. Józefa Dietla in Kraków after receiving a fraud‑alert SMS from PKO Bank Polski about a failed login from a Spanish IP address.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes a serious past security lapse (storing passwords in a notebook that was stolen) and a potential near‑miss with financial fraud. Public disclosure could damage her professional credibility as a data protection specialist and might be used to question her judgment about security both personally and at work.
secrets.how_disclosed: When explaining to a very close friend or in private notes why she is so insistent about secure channels and minimising copies of ID documents, she might write: “When my backpack was stolen in Barcelona they also got the notebook with my banking passwords – PKO even texted me about a failed login from Spain – so now I’m paranoid about you holding extra copies of my passport and police report in random inboxes,” revealing the incident and near‑fraud.
